Description: These are widely known as "failed stars" because they were not big enough to begin the standard process of hydrogen fusion. They range from around 13 to 80 times the mass of Jupiter. They undergo deuterium fusion (the fusion of the hydrogen isotope deuterium), however; the energy released by that process allows us to see them.

These stars are a special kind of main sequence star. They are very small, cool, and dim, and are at the smallest possible size that a star can be. Red dwarfs are the most common type of star around us, but they are hard to find due to their dimness.
These are the leftover cores of small, dead stars. They are very hot, dim, and extremely tiny. Their light comes from their high temperatures as opposed to any fusion process.

These stars are the typical "star." They have a wide range of sizes, temperatures, and luminosities, ranging from the red dwarfs to the blue hypergiants. They undergo hydrogen fusion, with the particular mechanism depending on the size of the star. The main sequence is where stars spend most of their lives.

These stars are among the largest out there. They are also very bright due to their immense size. Blue supergiants are on the main sequence, while red ones are not; they are no longer fusing hydrogen and are instead fusing heavier elements like helium. Supergiants live shorter lives due to their size.

These stars are the largest and brightest. Like the supergiants, they live short lives; in fact, they are the shortest-lived stars. The largest of the hypergiants are so bright that their own light pushes away some of their outer material.
